questions.text: Which of the following describes the fundamental cycle of process execution in a system?

choices.label: Memory allocation followed by CPU execution
choices.text: Memory allocation followed by CPU execution

choices.label: A cycle of CPU execution and I/O wait
choices.text: A cycle of CPU execution and I/O wait

choices.label: System call request and kernel interrupt
choices.text: System call request and kernel interrupt

questions.explanation: Process execution consists of a cycle of CPU execution (CPU burst) and I/O wait (I/O burst).

questions.text: Under which of the following circumstances is CPU scheduling considered **nonpreemptive**? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: When a process switches from the running state to the waiting state
choices.text: When a process switches from the running state to the waiting state

choices.label: When a process switches from the running state to the ready state
choices.text: When a process switches from the running state to the ready state

choices.label: When a process switches from the waiting state to the ready state
choices.text: When a process switches from the waiting state to the ready state

questions.explanation: Scheduling is nonpreemptive only when a process switches from running to waiting or when it terminates. In these cases, the process gives up the CPU voluntarily.

questions.text: What is the term for the time it takes for the dispatcher to stop one process and start another running?
questions.explanation: Dispatch latency is the specific time used by the dispatcher to stop one process and initiate another.

questions.text: Which scheduling criterion measures the number of processes that complete their execution per time unit?
questions.explanation: Throughput is the count of processes completed in a specific time frame.

questions.text: In a time-sharing environment, which metric is most critical for measuring the time from request submission until the first response is produced?
questions.explanation: Response time is the interval between a request being submitted and the first response being produced.

questions.text: What is the primary disadvantage of the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling algorithm?

choices.label: It causes high overhead for context switching
choices.text: It causes high overhead for context switching

choices.label: It may result in the convoy effect
choices.text: It may result in the convoy effect

questions.explanation: The convoy effect occurs in FCFS when short processes must wait for a single long, CPU-bound process to finish.

questions.text: Shortest-Job-First (SJF) scheduling is considered optimal because it:

choices.label: Minimizes average waiting time for a given set of processes
choices.text: Minimizes average waiting time for a given set of processes

questions.explanation: SJF is optimal because it reduces the average waiting time more than any other scheduling algorithm for a specific set of processes.

questions.text: Predicting the length of the next CPU burst in SJF is often done using exponential averaging. Given the formula \( \tau_{n+1} = \alpha t_n + (1 - \alpha)\tau_n \), what does \( \alpha = 0 \) imply?

choices.label: Only the most recent actual CPU burst counts
choices.text: Only the most recent actual CPU burst counts

choices.label: Recent history does not count toward the prediction
choices.text: Recent history does not count toward the prediction

choices.label: The prediction is equal to the average of all previous bursts
choices.text: The prediction is equal to the average of all previous bursts

choices.label: The system assumes the next burst will be zero
choices.text: The system assumes the next burst will be zero

questions.explanation: If \( \alpha = 0 \), then \( \tau_{n+1} = \tau_n \), meaning recent history (actual burst \( t_n \)) is ignored and only the predicted history is used.

questions.text: Shortest-remaining-time-first (SRTF) is the preemptive version of which algorithm?
questions.explanation: SRTF is the preemptive implementation of SJF, where the currently running process can be interrupted if a new process arrives with a shorter remaining burst time.

questions.text: Priority scheduling can lead to 'starvation'. Which technique is used to solve this problem?
questions.explanation: Aging gradually increases the priority of processes that wait in the system for a long time, preventing them from being indefinitely postponed (starvation).

questions.text: In Round Robin (RR) scheduling, what happens when the time quantum is very large?

choices.label: Context switch overhead becomes too high
choices.text: Context switch overhead becomes too high

questions.explanation: If the time quantum is larger than the longest CPU burst, RR effectively becomes FCFS.

questions.text: What is the recommended rule of thumb regarding the relationship between CPU bursts and the time quantum \( q \)?

choices.label: 20% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)
choices.text: 20% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)

choices.label: 50% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)
choices.text: 50% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)

choices.label: 80% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)
choices.text: 80% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)

choices.label: 99% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)
choices.text: 99% of CPU bursts should be shorter than \( q \)

questions.explanation: Turnaround time is improved when most processes (roughly 80%) finish their CPU burst within a single time quantum.

questions.text: Which scheduling algorithm partitions the ready queue into separate queues for foreground and background processes?
questions.explanation: Multilevel Queue scheduling divides processes into separate queues (like interactive and batch) based on their characteristics.

questions.text: What is the key difference between Multilevel Queue and Multilevel Feedback Queue scheduling?

choices.label: Multilevel Feedback Queue is only used in Windows
choices.text: Multilevel Feedback Queue is only used in Windows

choices.label: Multilevel Feedback Queue allows processes to move between queues
choices.text: Multilevel Feedback Queue allows processes to move between queues

choices.label: Multilevel Queue uses aging while Feedback Queue does not
choices.text: Multilevel Queue uses aging while Feedback Queue does not

choices.label: Multilevel Queue is preemptive while Feedback Queue is not
choices.text: Multilevel Queue is preemptive while Feedback Queue is not

questions.explanation: Unlike a simple Multilevel Queue where processes are permanently assigned to a queue, Multilevel Feedback Queues allow processes to move between them, enabling features like aging.

questions.text: In thread scheduling, Process-Contention Scope (PCS) refers to:

choices.label: Competition among all threads in the system
choices.text: Competition among all threads in the system

choices.label: Competition for the CPU within a single process
choices.text: Competition for the CPU within a single process

choices.label: The mapping of processes to I/O devices
choices.text: The mapping of processes to I/O devices

questions.explanation: PCS refers to scheduling user-level threads to run on available LWPs (lightweight processes) within the same process.

questions.text: Which of the following describes Asymmetric Multiprocessing?

choices.label: Only one processor (the master) accesses system data structures
choices.text: Only one processor (the master) accesses system data structures

choices.label: All processors share a common ready queue
choices.text: All processors share a common ready queue

choices.label: Processes are fixed to a single core
choices.text: Processes are fixed to a single core

questions.explanation: Asymmetric multiprocessing centralizes scheduling decisions and data structure access to one master processor to reduce complexity.

questions.text: What is the purpose of 'Load Balancing' in Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP) systems?

choices.label: To ensure each process has affinity for its processor
choices.text: To ensure each process has affinity for its processor

choices.label: To keep the workload evenly distributed across all processors
choices.text: To keep the workload evenly distributed across all processors

questions.explanation: Load balancing attempts to keep all CPUs equally busy using techniques like push and pull migration.

questions.text: Multithreaded multicore systems take advantage of 'memory stall' to:

choices.label: Shut down idle cores to save power
choices.text: Shut down idle cores to save power

choices.label: Make progress on another thread while one thread waits for memory
choices.text: Make progress on another thread while one thread waits for memory

questions.explanation: When a processor thread waits for memory access (memory stall), the hardware can switch to another thread to maintain throughput.

questions.text: What are the two types of latencies that affect real-time system performance? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Interrupt latency (time to reach the ISR) and dispatch latency (time to switch tasks) are critical to real-time performance.

questions.text: In Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), how are priorities assigned?

choices.label: Based on the length of the CPU burst
choices.text: Based on the length of the CPU burst

choices.label: Based on the inverse of the period (shorter periods get higher priority)
choices.text: Based on the inverse of the period (shorter periods get higher priority)

choices.label: Based on the deadline (earlier deadlines get higher priority)
choices.text: Based on the deadline (earlier deadlines get higher priority)

questions.explanation: RMS assigns static priorities where tasks that repeat more frequently (shorter periods) are given higher priority.

questions.text: Which scheduling algorithm allows for priorities to change dynamically based on the task's deadline?
questions.explanation: In EDF, the closer a task is to its deadline, the higher its priority becomes.

questions.text: The Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) calculates the next task to run based on which variable?
questions.explanation: CFS maintains a `vruntime` (virtual runtime) for each task and selects the task with the lowest `vruntime` to ensure fairness.

questions.text: How many priority levels are in the Windows 32-level priority scheme?
questions.explanation: Windows uses a 32-level scheme where 0 is for memory management, 1-15 are variable classes, and 16-31 are real-time classes.

questions.text: Which formula expresses Little's Law in a steady state?
questions.explanation: Little's Law states that the average number of processes in the queue (n) equals the average arrival rate (\( \lambda \)) multiplied by the average waiting time (W).

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of using 'Simulations' for algorithm evaluation over deterministic modeling?

choices.label: They provide exact numbers for simple inputs
choices.text: They provide exact numbers for simple inputs

choices.label: They are more accurate as they use a programmed model and probabilistic data
choices.text: They are more accurate as they use a programmed model and probabilistic data

questions.explanation: Simulations use programmed models of systems and can utilize trace tapes or random number generators to provide much higher accuracy than simple analytic models.
